does: This paper applies affordance theory to systematically analyze the OpenAI Preparedness Framework Version 2 (April 2025), examining what actions it actually enables, encourages, allows, or discourages regarding AI development and deployment. Using the Mechanisms & Conditions model of affordances and the MIT AI Risk Repository, the authors conduct a structured policy analysis that maps which AI risks receive evaluation attention and under what conditions deployment is permitted. Their core finding is that the framework permits deployment of systems with medium capabilities even when they present severe harm risks as defined by OpenAI itself (greater than 1000 deaths or $100 billion in damages), and further allows the CEO to deploy more dangerous capabilities without apparent constraint. The work demonstrates a significant gap between the framework's purported safety commitments and its actual governance effects.
connects: This work extends critical examination of industry self-governance in AI safety, responding to the growing gap between prominent company safety statements and actual risk mitigation practices. It builds on policy analysis literature by applying affordance theory—traditionally used in human-computer interaction and design—to understand the practical constraints and enablements embedded in written policies. The paper connects to prior work questioning the effectiveness of voluntary self-regulation in AI development, the limitations of company-defined risk thresholds, and concerns about governance captured by corporate interests. It also draws on scholarship examining how organizational structures and executive authority can override nominal safety procedures. By operationalizing affordance analysis through the MIT AI Risk Repository, the work bridges theoretical policy critique with concrete enumeration of actual safety coverage and permitted actions.
enables: This affordance-based analytical approach creates a replicable methodology for auditing safety policies from other AI companies and for comparative governance analysis across the industry. The work points toward more rigorous assessment frameworks for evaluating whether voluntary self-governance actually mitigates identified AI risks or merely provides reassurance. It establishes groundwork for future research examining the structural features of effective AI safety governance—such as external verification, enforceable thresholds, limitations on executive override, and mandatory coverage of documented risk classes. The findings support arguments for regulatory intervention beyond industry self-regulation and suggest that credible AI risk mitigation requires governance mechanisms with genuine enforcement capacity and constraint on corporate decision-making authority. The methodology could be extended to analyze corporate safety claims more broadly.
